name: Ebisu
description: Ebisu, also known as Hiruko, is one of the Seven Gods of Fortune (Shichi Fukujin) in Japanese mythology. He is often depicted with a high, pointed hat, holding a fishing rod and a tai (sea bream), symbolizing abundance and good fortune. Ebisu is the only one of the seven gods believed to have originated purely from Japan, without influence from Indian or Chinese deities. His origins are shrouded in myth, with some accounts claiming he was born deformed and cast out to sea. He eventually washed ashore and, after overcoming his physical disabilities, became a benevolent deity. Ebisu is revered as the god of fishermen, merchants, and prosperity. He is believed to bring good luck in business, safe voyages for sailors, and bountiful catches for fishermen. Shrines dedicated to Ebisu are often located near the sea or in commercial districts. He is often depicted as cheerful and approachable, a symbol of honest labor and fair dealing. Offerings of fish and sake are commonly made to Ebisu to ensure continued blessings of wealth and success. His association with the sea bream is particularly strong, making it a popular offering and a symbol of celebration. Ebisu's enduring popularity reflects the importance of commerce and the sea in Japanese culture.
subtitle: God of Fishermen, Prosperity, and Luck

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Hiruko
keyMyths.description: Ebisu, initially known as Hiruko, was born deformed as the first child of Izanagi and Izanami. Due to his deformities, he was cast adrift in a reed boat.

keyMyths.title: Ebisu's Arrival Ashore
keyMyths.description: After being cast adrift, Hiruko eventually washed ashore and was cared for by the Ainu people. He overcame his disabilities to become a powerful deity.

keyMyths.title: Ebisu and the Sea Bream
keyMyths.description: Ebisu is always depicted with a sea bream, which symbolizes good fortune and abundance. The sea bream is often offered to him at shrines.

sources.author: Donald Philippi
sources.description: Translation of the Kojiki, an early Japanese chronicle.
sources.text: Translation of the Kojiki, an early Japanese chronicle.

sources.author: Helen Hardacre
sources.description: Overview of Shinto beliefs and practices.

sources.text: Description of common Shinto practices and deities within Japanese homes.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, especially fish and rice, are commonly made at Ebisu shrines. Sake is also a frequent offering, symbolizing purity and respect.

worshipPractices.name: Ebisu-ko Festivals
worshipPractices.description: Participating in Ebisu-ko festivals, where communities gather to pray for prosperity and good catches. These festivals often involve lively parades and celebratory meals.

worshipPractices.name: Displaying Images
worshipPractices.description: Placing images or statues of Ebisu in homes and businesses, particularly near entrances or in areas where commerce takes place, to invite good fortune.

festivals.name: Toka Ebisu
festivals.description: A major festival held annually at Imamiya Ebisu Shrine in Osaka, celebrating Ebisu and praying for business prosperity in the new year. It features elaborate processions and the selling of lucky bamboo branches adorned with charms.

festivals.name: Ebisu-ko
festivals.description: Local festivals held throughout Japan, often involving gatherings of merchants and fishermen to honor Ebisu and pray for good fortune. These festivals usually include feasts, games, and the exchange of gifts.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ebisu remains a popular deity in modern Japan, particularly among business owners and fishermen. His image is frequently used in advertising and branding, symbolizing prosperity and good luck. Ebisu shrines continue to attract visitors seeking blessings for their ventures, demonstrating his enduring cultural significance.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Ebisu's origins are intertwined with the myth of Hiruko, the 'leech child.' According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the earliest chronicles of Japan, Hiruko was the first child of Izanagi and Izanami, the creator deities. However, due to Izanami's violation of ritual protocol during their union, Hiruko was born deformed and without bones. He was subsequently cast adrift in a reed boat, eventually washing ashore. Different regions have varying accounts of what happened to Hiruko after this event. Some believe he overcame his physical challenges and became the deity Ebisu, while others maintain that Ebisu is a separate deity altogether. This ambiguity surrounding his birth contributes to the mystique and unique position of Ebisu within the Shichi Fukujin.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Ebisu does not feature prominently in many major myths like some other deities, his stories often revolve around his perseverance and eventual rise to become a god of fortune. One common narrative depicts him as overcoming his initial physical disabilities to become a skilled fisherman. He is often portrayed as a cheerful and benevolent figure, bringing prosperity to those who worship him. Stories also emphasize his connection to the sea and maritime activities, highlighting his role as a protector of fishermen and sailors. The image of Ebisu holding a fishing rod and a tai (sea bream) is a recurring motif in these narratives, symbolizing abundance and good fortune. These tales reinforce his association with commerce, trade, and the daily lives of ordinary people.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Ebisu is widely worshipped throughout Japan, particularly by merchants, fishermen, and business owners. Shrines dedicated to Ebisu are common, and his image is frequently displayed in homes and businesses to attract good fortune. Worship practices often involve offerings of food, especially fish and rice, and prayers for prosperity and success. Ebisu-ko festivals, local celebrations held in his honor, are a significant part of his cult. These festivals typically involve gatherings of community members, feasts, and the exchange of gifts. The Toka Ebisu festival in Osaka is one of the most prominent events dedicated to Ebisu, attracting large crowds seeking blessings for their businesses in the new year. His worship reflects a deep-seated desire for economic stability and well-being.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Ebisu's legacy extends beyond religious worship, permeating various aspects of Japanese culture. As one of the Seven Gods of Fortune, he embodies values of hard work, perseverance, and the pursuit of prosperity. His image is frequently used in advertising and branding, symbolizing good luck and success. Ebisu's association with the sea and fishing has also made him a popular figure in coastal communities. His enduring appeal lies in his relatability and his connection to everyday life. Unlike some deities associated with distant or abstract concepts, Ebisu represents tangible benefits such as wealth, abundance, and safety. His continued presence in contemporary Japan demonstrates the lasting influence of traditional beliefs on modern society.

shortDescription: Ebisu is the Japanese god of fishermen, commerce, and good fortune, and one of the Seven Gods of Fortune. He is often depicted with a fishing rod and sea bream.
